nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, forest streams, floodplain lakes, and blackwater creeks; typically in shaded areas with abundant aquatic vegetation, leaf litter, and driftwood

decorPreferences: dark substrate to enhance the iridescent gold-orange spots
dense planting (Java fern, Amazon sword, hornwort, water sprite)
driftwood and roots for cover and tannin release
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves) for blackwater biotope authenticity
dim or diffused lighting to encourage natural behavior

diet.notes: Readily accepts a wide variety of foods. Staple diet of high-quality micro-pellets or flake food supplemented with small frozen and live foods such as brine shrimp, daphnia, bloodworms, and mosquito larvae. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, insects, and plant matter. Feed small amounts twice daily.

goodWith: other small tetras (neon, cardinal, rummy-nose, glowlight)
dwarf shrimp (with caution — adults generally safe)
avoidWith: large predatory fish that can swallow them
very small fry or nano fish that may be outcompeted

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo for Hemigrammus ocellifer. Shows the characteristic head and tail light spots clearly.
Wikimedia Commons image showing the species in aquarium setting; verify current availability.

summary: A classic, hardy community tetra distinguished by two iridescent gold-orange spots — one behind the eye (the 'headlight') and one at the caudal peduncle base (the 'taillight'). Highly adaptable, beginner-friendly, and one of the longest-established tetras in the hobby.
careNotes:
The Head-and-Tail-Light Tetra is one of the most established tetras in the aquarium hobby, having been exported to Europe as early as 1910. It is valued for its hardiness and adaptability across a wide range of water conditions, performing well in community tanks from soft acidic blackwater setups to moderately hard neutral water. Preferred parameters are soft to moderately hard water (gH 2–20) with a pH of 5.5–7.5 and temperature of 22–27°C, though it tolerates somewhat harder or warmer conditions than many Amazonian tetras.

A minimum group of 6 is recommended to elicit natural schooling behaviour; 8–10+ individuals produce the most visually striking shoaling effect and reduce timidity. A dark substrate dramatically intensifies the appearance of the two signature iridescent gold-orange spots — one situated just behind the operculum (the 'headlight') and one at the base of the caudal peduncle (the 'taillight'). These spots appear to glow under angled lighting. Driftwood, roots, and Indian almond leaves are beneficial, releasing tannins that soften the water and replicate the dark, tannin-stained streams of their native Guyana and Amazon basin habitat.

Subspecies note: The nominotypical form H. o. ocellifer bears both the opercular (head) spot and the caudal (tail) spot. Hermann Meinken described H. o. falsus in 1958 after noticing that most aquarium-traded individuals lacked the opercular spot; this form is now generally treated as a separate species (H. falsus). Virtually all specimens in the current trade are the true H. ocellifer with both spots present.

See also: [glowlight-tetra] (H. erythrozonus) — similar care, solid red stripe instead of spot markings; [rummy-nose-tetra] — another community tetra requiring pristine water quality.
breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner; moderately easy to breed with appropriate conditioning. Use a dedicated breeding tank of 40–60 L with soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–5, temperature 26–27°C) and no substrate or fine-leaved plants (Java moss, spawning mops) for egg adhesion. Dim the lighting to near-darkness to encourage spawning. Condition adults separately with live or frozen foods for 1–2 weeks before pairing. A female in breeding condition becomes visibly rounded. Spawning is egg-scattering; females produce 200–1000 eggs per spawning event. Adults must be removed immediately after spawning as they will consume the eggs. Eggs are adhesive and settle on plants or the tank bottom; they hatch within 24–36 hours. Fry are free-swimming in 3–4 days. Feed with infusoria or commercial fry foods initially, transitioning to baby brine shrimp and micro-worms as they grow.

primary.name: AquariumStoreDepot — Head and Tail Light Tetra Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade care source. Provided adult size (4.5–5 cm), temperature (22–27°C), pH (5.5–7.5), hardness (2–20 dGH), lifespan (3–5 years), minimum tank (20 gal / 76 L for 6 fish), diet, behavior, native range, breeding notes, and compatibility. Cross-referenced against FishBase (max 4.9 cm TL, 22–26°C, pH 6–8, native Guyana/Suriname/French Guiana/Amazon), fishkeeper.co.uk (22–26°C, pH 6–8, KH up to 18°, 5 cm, 200–1000 eggs/spawn), and tropical-fish-keeping.com (pH 5.5–7.5, dH 5–20, Amazon and Orinoco basins).

additional.name: FishBase — Hemigrammus ocellifer
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 4.9 cm TL), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6–8), native range (Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, Amazon basin in Brazil and Peru), diet (worms, small insects, crustaceans, plants), taxonomy (Characiformes), and gregarious behaviour (groups of 5+, min 60 cm aquarium).

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Hemigrammus ocellifer
additional.notes: Subspecies taxonomy notes: H. o. falsus described by Meinken 1958 as lacking the opercular (shoulder) spot; now considered a distinct species (H. falsus) that no longer mixes with H. ocellifer in nature. Virtually all current trade specimens are captive-bred H. ocellifer with both spots present. Species introduced to Germany in 1910.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk — Head & Tail Light Tetra
additional.notes: Additional confirmation of size (5 cm), temperature (22–26°C), pH (6–8), KH up to 18°, native range (rivers of Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, Amazon basin of Brazil and Peru), egg count (200–1000 eggs, hatch in 24 hrs), diet (flake, granules, frozen/live mosquito larvae, brine shrimp, daphnia), and minimum group of 6.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping — Head and Tail Light Tetra
additional.notes: Confirmed pH range 5.5–7.5, hardness dH 5–20, native range includes Amazon and Orinoco River basins, Guyana, Suriname, French Guiana, Peru and Brazil. Lifespan ~5 years. Described habitat: slow-moving rivers, streams, and floodplain lakes; black water biotope setups recommended.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scattering spawner producing 200–1000 eggs per spawn; eggs hatch in 24–36 hours. Requires a dedicated breeding tank with soft acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5), fine-leaved plants or spawning mops, dim lighting, and removal of adults immediately post-spawn to prevent egg predation. Fry accept infusoria then baby brine shrimp.
